turns.content: Imagine I'm building a proof-of-concept FFI wrapper where I need to pass raw memory pointers into C code without triggering any cleanup. If I just wrap the data in a normal struct, the Drop implementation will automatically run when the Rust side goes out of scope, which is exactly what I want to avoid right now.

You're describing a classic FFI pattern where you need to prevent automatic cleanup on the Rust side. The solution involves using ManuallyDrop to suppress the Drop trait and then explicitly calling mem::forget on that value when you hand off ownership to C, ensuring no destructor runs before the pointer is released.
That sounds straightforward but I'm worried about edge cases. What happens if someone tries to access the data after forgetting it? Won't that panic or cause undefined behavior?

You've caught a critical issue here. Once you call mem::forget on a ManuallyDrop, the value is completely gone from memory and no longer safe to inspect. Any attempt to dereference it afterward will trigger an unsafe block because you're treating dropped memory as if it still existed, which Rust flags with a compile error unless you explicitly mark it as unsafe.
So I can't just use regular pointers then? Do I have to manually implement the drop logic myself instead of relying on the compiler?

Exactly. You need to take manual control over memory management since standard types won't work for this scenario. Instead of letting the runtime handle cleanup, you create an unsafe wrapper that directly manipulates raw pointers and implements custom deallocation logic without using the Drop trait at all.
But wait, if I'm avoiding Drop entirely, how do I ensure memory gets freed when C returns control? Shouldn't there be some kind of guard or callback mechanism?

In pure Rust FFI scenarios like this, you typically don't use callbacks for cleanup because those mechanisms are reserved for async runtime contexts. Instead, the responsibility shifts to the caller side in C to manage memory reclamation using their own free function pointers.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about when ManuallyDrop actually gets used versus just regular unsafe blocks without any special wrapper types.

ManuallyDrop is specifically useful for wrapping existing owned values that you want to temporarily remove from the ownership graph. You use it around structs containing heap data, then call mem::forget on the entire wrapped value when transferring control to C, effectively removing all internal references at once without running Drop.

Okay so my flow would be: wrap existing owned values in ManuallyDrop, pass that through unsafe FFI code, and forget it before returning? Is there any scenario where I'd just use raw pointers directly instead?

Yes. If you're starting with already freed memory or managing external C allocations, raw pointers bypass the Drop system entirely. But for owned Rust data being transferred to C, ManuallyDrop combined with mem::forget is your standard approach because it lets you move existing heap-allocated values safely across boundaries.
I think I'm getting confused between forget and just dropping something early. Can you explain the difference more clearly? Like what's actually happening under the hood?

Forget explicitly removes a value from memory without triggering Drop, whereas dropping normally calls your destructor first then frees memory. Think of it like unloading a truck: forget skips the inspection step entirely and just drives away with nothing left behind, while drop inspects everything before leaving.
That analogy helps but I'm wondering about custom collections where we want to prevent cleanup during initialization or specific operations?

Custom collections often use ManuallyDrop internally to create temporary internal nodes that won't trigger cleanup until the entire structure is fully constructed. This prevents partial drops during complex construction sequences and allows you to control exactly when memory reclamation occurs.
So if I build a custom allocator with custom collection internals, I should avoid Drop for those specific nodes? And only run it at the end?

Correct. For custom allocators and internal node management, you suppress Drop until the entire allocation strategy is complete. This ensures your custom deallocation logic runs exactly when you intend without interference from standard destructors.
One last thing I want to clarify: does using ManuallyDrop make code harder to read or debug since it hides the actual cleanup?

It can complicate debugging temporarily because the memory won't show signs of being freed until you explicitly trace the forget call. However, once established in your FFI pattern, it becomes a standard convention that makes the intent clear to other developers working with low-level Rust code.

Hey, I'm trying to port an old C library into Rust for a new project. The original code uses pointers that need to stay alive across function boundaries without being freed automatically. I thought I'd just use regular Rc or RefCell, but I keep getting errors about double-free or memory corruption when the data is passed between layers.

That sounds like a classic pitfall when moving away from manual pointer management. You're likely hitting issues where ownership isn't being transferred correctly through boundaries. The solution involves using ManuallyDrop to prevent automatic cleanup and mem::forget to explicitly skip the Drop implementation for that specific instance. This is common in FFI scenarios or custom collection internals.
Right, so when I pass a pointer from a Rust function into C code and back again, the Rust side thinks it still owns memory that's actually been freed by C? That would cause a crash on return.

Exactly. Without ManuallyDrop, the compiler will call Drop on your borrowed data even if you've already handed ownership away or let C free it. You need to wrap the pointer in ManuallyDrop so Rust ignores its Drop implementation until you explicitly choose to release it again later.
Wait, does that mean I can never free that memory? Or do I have a way to manually reclaim it without triggering unsafe code?
You do. The key is using mem::forget on the ManuallyDrop wrapper after you're done with it. This tells Rust not to run Drop anymore and prevents double-free errors.
So if I have a Vec internally holding raw pointers, can I just forget those pointers instead of dropping them? Or should I keep them in a separate collection that drops safely?

If you forget individual items inside a Vec, the entire container might still try to drop its elements. You generally want to hold pointers in a custom internal structure where you manage their lifecycle yourself, avoiding automatic cleanup until needed.
Got it. So I'm building a wrapper struct that holds these raw pointers and manually tracks which ones need freeing? And I use ManuallyDrop for the actual pointer values inside?
That's exactly right. You can define your own internal type using unsafe blocks with ManuallyDrop to control when cleanup happens, ensuring safety while maintaining flexibility.
One more thing though: what if I need to pass this back into C but also want to keep some data in Rust? How do I ensure the Rust side doesn't try to free memory that C already handled?

You use ManuallyDrop on the raw pointer values and then apply mem::forget selectively only when you're ready to return ownership or transfer control. This ensures Rust never attempts to re-free memory.
Okay, so I wrap pointers in ManuallyDrop, expose them through FFI, and forget them before returning? But what about the rest of my data structures? Do they still need normal Drop behavior?

Yes. Only the specific raw pointer instances that are being transferred or managed externally should use this pattern. All other collections and owned data in your Rust code continue to rely on standard Drop semantics.
Perfect, I think I understand now. One last question: is there a crate that simplifies this so I don't have to write unsafe blocks manually?
While there are helper crates for FFI and memory management, the core pattern relies on ManuallyDrop and mem::forget directly from std. Most advanced wrappers just expose these primitives safely.
Thanks! That makes sense. I'll implement a simple wrapper using ManuallyDrop and test it with my C bindings before committing to production.
That's the right approach. Testing edge cases like double-free scenarios is crucial when dealing with manual memory management in Rust.
Sounds good. I'm going to start coding now and let you know if I hit any unexpected errors.
Feel free to reach out if things get tricky. Good luck with the port!
Actually, I ran into an error where my custom collection is panicking during cleanup even though I used ManuallyDrop on the pointers. What's going wrong?

If you're seeing panics after using ManuallyDrop correctly, check if any other parts of your data structure are still triggering Drop unexpectedly. Ensure that only the intended raw pointers have been wrapped and forgotten.
Ah yes! I forgot to apply mem::forget on some child elements before passing them up the hierarchy. That caused a double-free in the parent's drop logic.
Exactly. Consistency is key here. Every pointer instance that needs to be transferred or externally managed must have its Drop behavior explicitly disabled via forget.
Okay, I've fixed it by forgetting all necessary pointers before returning them from the function. Now the cleanup works as expected without panics.
Great! That's exactly how you handle this pattern correctly in Rust.
Thanks for walking me through this step-by-step. This was really helpful.
You're welcome! Just remember that ManuallyDrop and forget are powerful tools when used intentionally rather than as a workaround.
One final question: can I combine these techniques with other ownership patterns like Rc or Arc?
Yes, but be careful. If you mix manual memory management with shared references incorrectly, you risk undefined behavior. Use ManuallyDrop only when explicitly transferring control.
Understood. I'll keep the distinction clear between manually managed pointers and standard owned data in my design.
That's a solid mental model to have going forward.
Thanks again, this has cleared up a lot of confusion around memory safety.
Anytime! Happy coding with Rust!

Imagine I have a struct that wraps an FFI pointer, and during the review of my colleague's code they used ManuallyDrop to prevent Drop from running on that pointer. But then later in their implementation they manually call mem::forget on it instead.

That is a contradictory pattern you are describing, and it indicates a fundamental misunderstanding of how Rust handles ownership and destruction. If you wrap an FFI pointer in ManuallyDrop, the intent is to bypass the Drop trait's default behavior so that the pointer itself isn't automatically dropped when the struct goes out of scope. However, calling mem::forget on the contents of a ManuallyDrop wrapper is redundant because ManuallyDrop already prevents automatic dropping.

I thought that was how you handle large allocations in FFI scenarios where you need to manually free memory using an unsafe block before the struct is dropped. I assumed Mem::Forget would be necessary if I used ManuallyDrop.

You are conflating two different mechanisms. When wrapping raw pointers for FFI, you typically use ManuallyDrop to prevent automatic destruction of the pointer value itself. The actual memory management happens via unsafe functions that explicitly deallocate the underlying data. Calling mem::forget on a ManuallyDrop wrapper is redundant because ManuallyDrop already stops the Drop trait from running.
But I read somewhere that you can use ManuallyDrop to create a custom collection where elements are never dropped automatically, so maybe I need Mem::Forget for each element?

You might be thinking of scenarios like implementing a custom iterator or a specific data structure where you want to prevent automatic cleanup. However, even in those cases, ManuallyDrop is the primary tool used to stop Drop from running. Calling mem::forget on the contents of a ManuallyDrop wrapper serves no additional purpose because the wrapper already achieves what you want.
So if I have a Vec<T> where T implements some special trait and I want to prevent Drop from running, should I just use ManuallyDrop?

Yes, for preventing automatic destruction of values like raw pointers or specific types in FFI contexts, you would wrap them in ManuallyDrop. The key concept here is that ManuallyDrop ensures the value stays alive beyond its normal scope lifetime without needing explicit mem::forget calls.
Wait, I remember seeing code where someone wrapped a pointer in ManuallyDrop and then called Mem::Forget on it anyway. That seemed wrong to me but maybe there was a reason?

That pattern is likely incorrect or represents a misunderstanding of scope rules. If you have already used ManuallyDrop correctly, calling mem::forget again would be redundant and could lead to double-free errors if not careful about the exact ownership semantics.
I am still confused because in some unsafe code examples I saw Mem::Forget being called on a borrowed slice, but that seems dangerous. Is there ever a case where you need both?

You are correct to be cautious; calling mem::forget on a borrowed reference is generally unsafe and leads to double-free vulnerabilities. The only valid use case for combining these concepts involves raw pointers wrapped in ManuallyDrop, where the goal is to prevent automatic deallocation of the pointer itself while retaining manual control over underlying memory.
Ah, I see now. So if I have a Box<T> and I want to convert it into a raw pointer without dropping T, do I need Mem::Forget?

No, you do not need mem::forget in that scenario. Converting a Box<T> to a raw pointer via an unsafe cast inherently avoids the Drop implementation of T because the ownership is transferred directly to the raw pointer representation.
But what if I have a custom collection where elements are pointers and I want them to persist until explicitly freed? Should I use ManuallyDrop?

Yes, for that specific use case in your custom collection implementation, wrapping each element pointer in ManuallyDrop is the correct approach. This prevents automatic destruction of the pointers when the collection goes out of scope, allowing you to manage their lifetime manually.
Okay, so I think my mistake was assuming Mem::Forget adds extra safety or control on top of ManuallyDrop. It actually just does nothing useful in most cases?

Exactly, your intuition is correct. The redundancy arises from misunderstanding how Rust's ownership system works with these primitives. ManuallyDrop already prevents the Drop trait from running, so mem::forget provides no additional benefit and can confuse the compiler or lead to logical errors.
One last thing: what happens if I forget to use ManuallyDrop but try to prevent Drop anyway?

If you omit ManuallyDrop and attempt to bypass automatic destruction, the code will likely panic at runtime when Drop is invoked or cause undefined behavior through double-free errors. The entire purpose of ManuallyDrop is to provide a safe way to opt out of this automatic lifecycle management.
